summary: DeVault describes his experience building agunix, a full Linux distribution from scratch, motivated by dissatisfaction with every existing distro. He distinguishes a Linux 'system' from a 'distribution' — the latter requiring a package manager and self-hosting capability. The bootstrapping process took multiple failed attempts over months, involving cross-compilation, dependency resolution, and countless edge cases. After achieving self-hosting on his fourth attempt, he graduated from a chroot to dedicated hardware, then deployed the distro's own infrastructure on itself. He notes the eternal packaging phase is the real bottleneck, having built 407 packages without yet achieving a desktop environment.
key_points: A Linux distribution differs from a Linux system by requiring a package manager and self-hosting capability — it must be able to compile itself
Bootstrapping a distro is brutally difficult with countless edge cases; DeVault succeeded on his fourth attempt and then redid it for cleanliness
His dissatisfaction spans all existing options: popular distros have bloat and GNU/systemd, niche distros are incomplete, and non-Linux kernels lack hardware support or POSIX compliance
The 'eternal packaging phase' is the true long tail — 407 packages built and still no desktop environment
Community buy-in is essential because it's impossible for one person to package everything
money_quotes: After trying a great number of distros and coming away with a kind of dissatisfaction unique to each one, I resolved to make a distro that embodied my own principles about userspace design.
Simply getting this software to run in a new Linux system is only moderately difficult - getting a system that can build itself is much harder.
It turns out this is a ton of work - here's how it's done.
I was successful on my 4th attempt, but threw it out and redid it to get a cleaner distribution with the benefit of hindsight.
I deliberately chose not to have a website until it could be hosted on agunix itself.
I have made 407 packages to date and I still don't have a desktop to show for it.
At this point to have success you need others to buy into your ideas and start contributing - it's impossible to package everything yourself.
plan9/9front has the most elegant kernel and userspace design ever made, but it's not POSIX and has shit hardware support.
key_insight: Building a real Linux distribution — one that is self-hosting and distributable — is orders of magnitude harder than assembling a bootable Linux system, requiring months of bootstrapping work and an essentially infinite packaging commitment.
